text:
NYCPS created the College and Career Readiness metric to replace the Career Readiness Index. The effort tracks the NYSED CCCR College, Career, and (?Community) Readiness value, which will be used for grauation certification. Students are awarded points out of 100 for milestones that align with college and career readiness, such as AP exam scores and apprenticeships.
Load and inspect the school quality dataset. Dataset is not available through the nycschools package, and is large for the SODA Api. Exported manually from https://data.cityofnewyork.us/Education/School-Quality-Reports-Data/dnpx-dfnc/

from pathlib import Path
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv(Path.cwd() / "../.env")

True

from nycschools import schools

dataloader.write_file(school_demographics, path='../data/school_demographics.csv')

# Get all of the variable display name pairs to lookup in the Educator's Guide. https://infohub.nyced.org/docs/default-source/default-document-library/finalized-2024-25-educator-guide-hs-1.pdf
metrics = school_quality_df[["Metric Variable Name","Metric Display Name"]].drop_duplicates()
ccr_cri_metrics = pandas.concat([metrics[metrics["Metric Variable Name"].str.contains("cri")],metrics[metrics["Metric Variable Name"].str.contains("ccr")]])


ccr_cri_df = school_quality_df[school_quality_df["Metric Variable Name"].isin(ccr_cri_metrics["Metric Variable Name"])]


# merge school quality metrics with demographics

    left=ccr_cri_df,
    right=school_demographics_df,
    left_on=["District, Borough and School Number (DBN)", "School Year"],
    right_on=["dbn", "ay"]

def my_filter(df, mask):
    """Apply mask to df"""
    return df[mask]
schools_with_girls = my_filter(combined_df, combined_df["female_pct"] >= 0.05)
schools_with_girls = schools_with_girls[["ay", "female_pct"]]

title: learning-plan.org
text:
Technical & Statistical Depth + Domain Fluency Benchmarks Non-AI-Assisted Verification Standard | 24-Month Framework | NYCPS Focus


Every benchmark in this document must be met without AI assistance. That is the test. You can use AI to learn, to check your work, and to accelerate exploration. You cannot use it to pass a gate. The difference between knowing something and being able to retrieve it via Claude is the difference between a senior analyst and a liability.



This plan is organized into four phases spanning 24 months. Each phase has a gate: a set of non-AI-assisted tasks you must complete before proceeding. The gates are not theoretical—they are the things a hiring manager will ask you to demonstrate in an interview or on a skills assessment. The plan is built around three parallel tracks that develop simultaneously:

Technical depth: Python, R, SQL, and reproducible research workflow
Statistical literacy: The ability to select, apply, and explain methods correctly
Domain fluency: NYCPS-specific knowledge that makes your analysis credible and legible to education stakeholders




Revenue is the inescapable constraint facilitating everything else. This must be named explicitly in the plan and applied as a filter on every planning decision.
What this means in practice:

Every week must have at least one task with a plausible revenue path: a client deliverable, a portfolio artifact that supports a job application, a skill that directly increases earning capacity, or a prototype that could become a product or service
Exploratory work (including prototypes like classroom-manager) is not banned, but it must be budgeted explicitly and must not displace revenue-oriented work by default
The learning plan’s 24-month arc is correct, but it will not produce income for 12–18 months without deliberate acceleration or parallel income sources

Revenue paths in order of proximity:

Freelance data analysis or consulting work using current skills (R/Quarto, NYCPS domain fluency, school data) — actionable now
Portfolio-driven job applications — actionable in 3–6 months with the CCR project as the anchor
classroom-manager as a product — uncertain timeline; requires technical validation, a customer, and a pricing model before it deserves significant time

Discipline rule: If a deep-work session is being pulled toward something with no near-term revenue path, name it, budget it (maximum 2 hours/week), and return to plan.




Structured tasks with explicit deadlines work. The R audit was difficult to start but effective once begun. This is the model for all future gate tasks.
Interesting ideas can override the plan entirely. classroom-manager consumed most of one week’s deep-work time. This is a real execution risk.
Working with a tutor provides the structure that converts ideas into starts. Without it, starting is the failure mode.
Deep work is available and real. The quality of focus once engaged is high. The problem is direction, not capacity.







R audit completed under timed, no-AI conditions (2 hours, with reference documentation only). Results recorded and published at posts/2026_03_20__r_audit/index.qmd.
classroom-manager multi-agent prototype initiated: a role-based agent system treating teaching as team-shaped work, testing whether agent handoffs can reduce teacher glue work across planning, curriculum, assessment, and communication. Unpublished and exploratory. Revenue path not yet established.





R + Quarto workflow: operational, reproducible, supports rendered analysis posts
CSV loading and tidyverse inspection: Strong (confirmed under audit)
Quarto render to HTML with inline R: Strong (confirmed under audit)
Starting and sustaining structured tasks once begun: strong, given a deadline
NYCPS domain knowledge: sufficient for analysis work; needs written recall practice for interview readiness





dplyr pipeline fluency: Shaky — takes too long, too many micro-decisions, inconsistent syntax recall
ggplot2: Shaky — can produce basic output; labels, scales, facets require reference
Linear regression in R: Gap — lm() not reached in audit; written interpretation of summary() output not practiced
Statistics writing from memory: Gap — p-value, confidence interval, effect size, regression coefficient interpretation not tested
SQL under independent recall: not yet audited
Python under independent recall: not yet audited
NYCPS domain knowledge from memory (not recognition): not yet tested





SQL audit (1 hour, no AI)
Statistics audit (written, 45 minutes, no AI)
Domain audit (written, 30 minutes, no AI)
Linear regression in R: complete lm() task and write interpretation of summary() output (can be appended to existing audit post)




Phase 0 is nearly complete for R and Quarto. The remaining gap is linear regression. SQL, statistics, and domain audits are the next three concrete tasks. Each is short. Phase 0 can close within 1–2 weeks if revenue-oriented work runs in parallel rather than these audits being treated as prerequisites for everything else.
Do not let Phase 0 become a holding pattern. The CCR project and portfolio work can begin in parallel with audit completion.

text:
Before You Begin
Establish an honest baseline. No learning yet. Just measurement.

title: learning-plan.org
text:
The Audit Tasks
Do each of these without AI assistance. Record where you get stuck — that's your Phase 1 curriculum.

Python Audit (2 hours, no AI, no Stack Overflow)

Load a CSV of NYCPS data into a pandas DataFrame
Filter rows by a condition, group by a column, compute a mean
Join two DataFrames on a common key
Handle a column with mixed types or missing values
Plot a bar chart with proper axis labels and a title
Write a function that takes a DataFrame and returns a summary
Connect to a local PostgreSQL database and run a query



R Audit (2 hours, no AI)

Load a CSV and inspect its structure using tidyverse
Write a dplyr pipeline: filter, select, mutate, groupby, summarize
Produce a ggplot2 bar chart with correct labels
Run a linear regression, call summary(), and explain each component of the output in writing
Render a Quarto document with inline R output to HTML


Results — Completed 2026-03-24 14:00-16:00
Difficult to start but productive once begun. Debate over approach added time and complexity beyond what the task required.

SQL Audit (1 hour, no AI)

Write a query with a JOIN, a WHERE clause, and a GROUP BY
Write a subquery
Explain what an index does and when you'd add one



Statistics Audit (written, 45 minutes)
Answer these in writing, as if explaining to a smart non-statistician:

What does a p-value actually mean? What does it NOT mean?
A school's test scores went up 5 points. Is that meaningful? What would you need to know?
What is the difference between correlation and causation? Give an example from education data.
What is chronic absenteeism? How is it calculated? Why does the 20% threshold matter?



Domain Audit (written, 30 minutes)

Name and describe five data systems used in NYCPS
Explain how a school's Quality Review score is determined
What is a NYSSIS ID and why does it exist?
List the demographic subgroups required for ESSA reporting
What is a D75 school?

title: learning-plan.org
text:
Audit Output
After completing the audit, score yourself: Strong / Shaky / Gap for each area. The gaps are your Phase 1 priorities. Be ruthless. Shaky means you needed to think too hard or hedged your answer. Gap means you couldn't do it.

title: learning-plan.org
text:
Phase 0 Exit Criteria — Updated 2026-03-27
Phase 0 is complete when all of the following are true:

You have written Strong / Shaky / Gap ratings for R, Python, SQL, statistics, and domain knowledge
You have at least one real artifact demonstrating your current R / Quarto workflow ✓ (r_audit post)
You can name the next one or two weakest areas without hedging ✓ (linear regression, statistics writing)
Your weekly plan stops trying to advance every area equally
The linear regression R task is completed and written up

title: learning-plan.org
section: Close the Gaps, Narrow the Stack
text:
Close the Gaps, Narrow the Stack
Achieve reliable, unassisted competence in a focused core. No new tools. No side projects.

What you are deferring
Emacs configuration, Positron exploration, Django feature development, new Kaggle competitions, gh-pages customization. These are real, useful things. They are Phase 2 and 3. Every hour spent on them in Phase 1 is a debt against your foundation.
Also explicitly deferred: classroom-manager prototype development beyond a bounded 2-hour/week exploration budget. It is a real idea. It is not a revenue source yet. It does not belong in the primary work track until it has a customer, a pricing model, or a direct path to income.


Stack Decision: What You Are Committing To
For Phase 1, your working stack is exactly this, nothing else:

What you must do without AI assistance
Verification

Python with pandas, matplotlib, seaborn, SQLAlchemy
Can produce output from scratch

Can produce output from scratch

Can render from scratch

Can connect, query, load

Git + GitHub (command line, not GUI)
Can commit, branch, push, resolve conflict

title: learning-plan.org
text:
Phase 1 Immediate Priority Order — Updated 2026-03-27
For the next several weeks, prioritize in this order:

Complete Phase 0 remaining audits: linear regression in R, SQL, statistics, domain — each is short; all should be done within one week
Begin CCR project in parallel — do not wait for perfect audit completion; the CCR project is the primary portfolio and revenue-path artifact
Convert current R/Quarto momentum into repeatable, no-AI statistical workflow fluency — practice dplyr and ggplot2 through the CCR analysis work itself, not through drills
Restore Python and SQL recall through the CCR pipeline — build the Python CCR data pipeline as the Python fluency exercise

title: learning-plan.org
text:
Phase 1 Statistics Benchmarks
Statistics is the area where novice analysts most commonly fake competence. These benchmarks are designed to expose that. Each must be demonstrated in writing or verbally without notes.

Core Concepts — Must Explain Correctly to a Non-Statistician



Benchmark
What you must do without AI assistance
Verification


Mean vs. median
When does mean mislead? Give an education data example where median is more honest.
Verbal


Standard deviation
Explain what 1 SD means in a distribution of school attendance rates.
Written


Confidence interval
What does a 95% CI actually mean? What does it NOT mean?
Written


p-value
Explain correctly — most analysts explain this wrong. What is the null hypothesis? What does p < .05 actually say?
Written


Effect size
Why is a statistically significant result not necessarily meaningful? Explain Cohen's d with a test score example.
Written


Correlation
What is r = .3 between attendance and test scores telling you? What can't you conclude?
Verbal


Regression coefficient
In a model predicting graduation rate from attendance and SES, interpret a coefficient of 0.4 on attendance.
Written


Confounding
Give two examples from education data where a confound changes the interpretation of a finding.
Written





Applied Statistics: You Must Be Able to Do These

Run a simple and multiple OLS regression in both Python (statsmodels) and R (lm()); interpret output identically from both
Check regression assumptions: linearity, homoscedasticity, normality of residuals, no multicollinearity — and know what to do when each is violated
Run a chi-square test of independence on categorical education data; interpret the result
Calculate and interpret Pearson and Spearman correlation; know when to use each
Distinguish between descriptive, inferential, and predictive analysis — and know which is appropriate for a given stakeholder question
Recognize when a dataset is too small for the claims being made

title: learning-plan.org
section: Political & Organizational Context — This Is What Separates Analysts
text:
Political & Organizational Context — This Is What Separates Analysts
A technically correct analysis that ignores organizational reality is useless. You must be able to speak to:

Why school-level data comparisons are almost always misleading without context (demographics, school type, selection effects)
The difference in how principals vs. superintendents vs. central office staff use data — and what they distrust
Why teachers are often skeptical of attendance data and what legitimate complaints they have
Which metrics are known to be gamed and why — and how to account for that in analysis
The history of mayoral control and how it shapes DOE data governance
Charter vs. district data comparisons: what's valid, what's not, why it's politically charged

title: learning-plan.org
section: Phase 1 Gate — You Must Pass All Three Before Phase 2
text:
Phase 1 Gate — You Must Pass All Three Before Phase 2

GATE 1A — TECHNICAL
Complete a data analysis of NYCPS public attendance data from raw download to published Quarto report. No AI assistance. Includes: data cleaning pipeline in Python, summary statistics, one regression, one visualization in both Python and R, written interpretation of findings. Time limit: one full working day.


GATE 1B — STATISTICAL
Write a two-page methodology memo explaining the statistical approach used in your Gate 1A analysis. Justify every choice. Acknowledge every limitation. A statistician should be able to read it and not find errors of interpretation.


GATE 1C — DOMAIN
Produce a one-page data dictionary for the dataset used in Gate 1A. Every field defined. Every known data quality issue noted. Every metric explained in terms a principal could understand. Do this from knowledge, not by copying documentation.

title: learning-plan.org
text:
Specialization and Portfolio Construction
Develop genuine analytical depth in education data. Publish substantial, reproducible analyses. These are the published analyses that constitute your portfolio. Each must be reproducible, documented, and publicly accessible on GitHub Pages or a Quarto Pub site.

You Must Be Able to Explain and Detect:

Simpson's Paradox — give an education data example and demonstrate it in data
Ecological fallacy — why school-level correlations can't be applied to students
Selection bias — why schools that opted into a program are not comparable to those that didn't
Regression to the mean — why the lowest-performing schools 'improve' without intervention
Ceiling and floor effects — why standardized tests compress variance at the extremes
Multiple comparisons problem — what happens when you test 30 hypotheses at p < .05

Logistic regression: predict a binary outcome (e.g., graduated yes/no); interpret log-odds correctly

title: learning-plan.org
text:
Phase 2 Portfolio: The CCR Project
One project, four published outputs. Each must be reproducible, documented, and publicly accessible on GitHub Pages or Quarto Pub.

OUTPUT 1 — CCR Metric Reconstruction Pipeline
Reproduce the CCR score from publicly available citywide results data using the Appendix A reference tables from the Educator Guide. Document every decision point: how the six scoring categories interact, how the associate degree category overrides others, how the six-category cap works, how suppression rules (n=15 general, n=10 ELL/SWD grad rates, n=5 lowest third citywide) are applied. Deliverable: a Python pipeline that takes school-level input and outputs CCR scores, validated against published values.


OUTPUT 2 — Comparison Group Approximation
You will not have student-level data to run the full value-added model. Be explicit about that. Use publicly available school-level characteristics (ENI, % ELL, % SWD, % by race, prior graduation rates) to build a regression approximating the comparison group methodology. Compare estimates to published comparison group values. The point is not to match perfectly — it is to demonstrate you understand what the model is doing and where your approximation breaks down. Deliverable: a documented regression with honest residual analysis and a clear limitations section.


OUTPUT 3 — Stakeholder Dashboard
A Quarto dashboard or Shiny app for a principal or network data manager. User selects a school and sees: CCR score vs. comparison group estimate; decomposition by the six CCR categories; comparison to borough and citywide averages; racial subgroup breakdowns for CCPCI and PSER where available; year-over-year trend if the data supports it. Deliverable: a live, published, interactive dashboard. Tables via Great Tables (Python) or gt (R) — the people reading this are principals and superintendents; beautiful tables matter. |


OUTPUT 4 — Policy Memo
One page, written for a superintendent or Student Pathways director. What does the CCR metric reward? What does it miss? Where do the incentives diverge from the stated goals of NY State's new graduation standards? What should a school leader attending to the broader rollout understand that the metric does not surface? This output demonstrates judgment, not just technical skill — it is often the one that gets you the interview. |

title: learning-plan.org
section: Metric Variable Name Filtering — Working Reference
text:
Metric Variable Name Filtering — Working Reference
When filtering the citywide school quality data for CCR-related metrics, use explicit named patterns rather than a single complex regex. The data's ~1.49M rows span Report Years 2016–2025; CCR metrics appear from 2024 onward.
ccr_patterns = [
    r'^ccr_',       # CCR score metrics (e.g., ccr_4y_all, ccr_4yr_l3c_hs)
    r'^ccpci_',     # College and Career Preparatory Course Index
    r'^pser_',      # Postsecondary Enrollment Rate
    r'^coll_pers',  # College persistence
]

combined = '|'.join(ccr_patterns)
ccr_data = data[data["Metric Variable Name"].str.contains(combined, regex=True, na=False)]
Prefer this over a single clever pattern: it is readable, maintainable, and self-documenting to a reviewer.

title: learning-plan.org
text:
Phase 2 Additional Portfolio Ideas
These are the three published analyses that constitute your portfolio. Each must be reproducible, documented, and publicly accessible on GitHub Pages or a Quarto Pub site.

Portfolio Idea — Chronic Absenteeism Analysis
A longitudinal analysis of chronic absenteeism rates across NYC schools, 2018–present (including COVID disruption). Borough/district breakdowns. Demographic subgroup comparisons with appropriate caveats. Regression identifying school-level predictors. Written for a superintendent audience. Methodology appendix written for a research audience. These are two different documents. |


Portfolio Idea — Regents & Graduation
An analysis of cohort graduation rates and Regents passage by school type (district, charter, transfer, D75). Honest about what the data can and cannot show. Includes a clearly stated limitations section. Visualizations appropriate for a policy brief. At least one finding that is counterintuitive or that challenges a common narrative.

text:

Pick a question a specific real stakeholder (principal, parent coordinator, network data manager) has actually asked you or that you know they care about. Design the analysis to answer their question, not to demonstrate your techniques. Publish both the analysis and a one-page plain-language summary. This piece demonstrates judgment, not just skill. |

All four CCR project outputs published. Each includes: a reproducible code repository, a rendered Quarto document or live dashboard, a plain-language summary, and a methodology note. Submit to one peer — ideally someone with education research background — for a written critique. Incorporate at least three substantive changes based on that feedback before proceeding to Phase 3. Target: a working version of all four outputs within 8–10 weeks of starting Phase 2.

title: learning-plan.org
text:
Credentialing, Network, and Income
Convert competence into verifiable credentials and market-facing presence.

Credentialing Strategy
Credentials matter in institutional education hiring. Your published portfolio is your primary credential, but it needs to be legible to HR gatekeepers. The following are the minimum threshold:

GitHub profile with consistent activity, clean repositories, and READMEs that explain what each project does
A personal site or Quarto Pub profile that presents the portfolio as a coherent body of work, not just a list of files
At least one piece of work cited or referenced outside your own channels — a blog post by someone else, a conference poster, a mention in a district newsletter
A one-page technical resume that lists specific tools and specific outputs, not generic skill claims



On Formal Degrees
Many senior analyst postings require an MA or EdD in education research, policy, or a related field. Your NYCPS experience and published portfolio can substitute for this in some organizations — charter networks, ed-tech companies, and nonprofits are more flexible than large districts. If your target is a DOE central office or research role, a graduate credential will likely be required eventually. Don't let this paralyze Phase 3, but plan for it honestly. |


Target Role Profiles
These are the roles you are positioning for. Understanding their differences shapes what you emphasize in your portfolio and how you describe yourself.



Benchmark
What you must do without AI assistance
Verification




Data Analyst, Charter Network
Smaller team, broader scope, faster-moving. High value on Python/SQL fluency, dashboard building, quick turnaround. Domain knowledge valued but not assumed.
Most accessible entry point


Research Analyst, NYC DOE
Slower hiring, more formal, often requires prior DOE experience or graduate credentials. Values methodological rigor and knowledge of DOE sy
18–36 month target; credential likely needed


Senior Analyst, Education Nonprofit
stems. Policy-facing. Values communication as much as technical skill. Published work is a strong differentiator. Smaller orgs more flexible on credentials.
Strong fit for your profile


Data Strategist / Manager
Hybrid technical-leadership role. Requires both technical depth and ability to advise others. Usually requires several years of analyst experience first.
Phase 4 target, not Phase 3


Ed-Tech Company (data/analytics)
Most similar to tech industry norms. Strong Python/SQL emphasis. Domain knowledge a differentiator over generalist candidates. Often remote.
Good fit; portfolio more important than credentials





Network Strategy
In education data, the field is small enough that network is decisive. The following are not optional:

Attend at least one NYAS, SREE, or AERA conference in Phase 3 — not to present, to meet people

New York Academy of Sciences Society for Research on Educational Effectiveness American Education Research Association

Identify 5–8 people doing the work you want to do. Read their published work. Engage with it in writing. Ask a specific, non-generic question.
Connect with NYCPS data managers and research directors you've interacted with as a teacher. A warm introduction from a former colleague is worth more than 20 cold applications.
Post about your published work — briefly, specifically, with a link. LinkedIn is not beneath you. One specific post about a finding is better than a profile update.
The Office of Student Pathways is an explicit target audience for the CCR project. If the dashboard or memo is good, it is a direct conversation starter with the people iterating on the metric.

title: learning-plan.org
text:
Phase 3 Gate — Readiness Criteria

GATE 3 — MARKET READINESS
You are ready to apply when: (1) The CCR project is published and has been read and critiqued by at least one person outside your network. (2) You can walk through any output in a 20-minute technical screen without notes. (3) You can answer 'what are the limitations of this analysis?' before the interviewer asks. (4) You have applied for at least 3 positions and completed at least 1 technical screen — even if you are not ready. The screen will tell you more than this document can. |

title: learning-plan.org
section: AI Use Policy for This Plan
text:
AI Use Policy for This Plan
This is the working rule: AI is a tutor, not a ghostwriter.

AI-permitted use: Ask it to explain a concept, check your understanding of a definition, suggest resources, review your written explanation of a method
AI-permitted use: After you've written code that works, ask it to critique or optimize it
AI-permitted use: Rubber duck debugging — describe a problem to AI after you've genuinely tried to solve it
AI-prohibited use: Writing any code that goes into a portfolio piece before you understand it line by line
AI-prohibited use: Producing any written analysis, methodology note, or data dictionary that you cannot regenerate independently
AI-prohibited use: Passing any gate described in this document

title: learning-plan.org
text:
The Self Deception Risk
The specific failure mode to watch for: you use AI to build something, it works, you feel competent, but you haven't actually learned anything. Six months later you're in an interview and can't explain your own portfolio. This is the most common and most damaging misuse of AI as a learning tool. The antidote is to rebuild things from scratch after AI helps you understand them — not once, but repeatedly.

title: Groupby
text:
Group by creates an object. When I apply an aggregator like median I get a data frame where the index labels are the unique values from the groupby column. Check and fill out my understanding.
Nice. Here’s a concise check and some fill-ins for GroupBy in pandas.
Core ideas - GroupBy creates a lazy GroupBy object. It doesn’t compute anything until you apply an aggregation or similar operation. - When you apply an aggregator (e.g., median, mean, sum) across numeric columns, you get a result whose rows correspond to the groups (the unique values of the groupby keys). - The index of the result is the grouping key(s). If you group by a single column, the index is that column’s unique values. If you group by multiple columns, you get a MultiIndex.
What you probably want to know about the “index labels” - For a single group key: - df.groupby(‘group’).median() yields a DataFrame whose index is the unique values of df[‘group’] (e.g., A, B, C). - The columns are the numeric columns you aggregated (e.g., ‘x’, ‘y’). - If you want the grouping key to be a regular column in the result: - Use as_index=False: df.groupby(‘group’, as_index=False).median() - Or call .reset_index() on the result: df.groupby(‘group’).median().reset_index()
Multiple grouping keys - df.groupby([‘dept’, ‘year’]).median() will produce a DataFrame indexed by a MultiIndex with two levels (dept, year). - To bring the keys back as columns: df.groupby([‘dept’, ‘year’], as_index=False).median() or .reset_index() after the result.
Examples - Basic single-group example: - df = DataFrame({‘team’: [‘A’,‘A’,‘B’,‘B’,‘C’], ‘score’: [10,20,30,40,50], ‘hours’: [1,2,3,4,5]}) - df.groupby(‘team’).median() - Result: index A, B, C; columns ‘score’ and ‘hours’ with medians per team.

Keep the group key as a column:

df.groupby(‘team’, as_index=False).median()
Result: a DataFrame with columns [‘team’, ‘score’, ‘hours’] and one row per team.


Common variations - To apply different functions to different columns: - df.groupby(‘group’).agg({‘score’: ‘median’, ‘hours’: ‘mean’}) - To get a single summary for one column: - df.groupby(‘group’)[‘score’].median() # returns a Series with group keys as the index
Common gotchas - Only numeric columns participate in median. Non-numeric columns are ignored in the aggregation (unless you specify otherwise). - Missing values are skipped by default (median ignores NaN). You can control this with skipna (if you need to).
Quick exercise for you - Given a DataFrame with columns [‘region’, ‘quarter’, ‘sales’, ‘cost’]: - How would you compute the median of ‘sales’ per ‘region’ and return the region as a column in the result? - How would you compute the same medians but preserve a MultiIndex if you grouped by both ‘region’ and ‘quarter’?
If you want, paste a small sample of your data and I’ll walk through the exact code for your case. Groupby is said to contain the split, transform, apply pattern. Elaborate on this pattern with the examples given above.
Great question. The groupby workflow is often described as the split-apply-combine (SAC) pattern. A related, handy specialization is split-apply-transform. Here’s how it maps to what you’ve been looking at and to the examples we covered.
What the SAC pattern means - Split: You partition the data into groups based on one or more keys. For example, df.groupby(‘group’) splits rows by the values in the group column. - Apply: You run a function on each group. This function is typically an aggregation (mean, median, sum) or a more general operation. - Combine: You stitch the per-group results back into a single object (a DataFrame or Series). The group labels usually become the index of the result (or they can be turned into columns).
How this shows up in your examples - Single grouping key with median - Split: data are split by the unique values in the group column. - Apply: the median is computed for each numeric column within each group. - Combine: a new DataFrame is produced whose rows correspond to the groups (A, B, C) and whose columns are the numeric aggregations (e.g., score, hours). The result’s index is the group values. - Code: df.groupby(‘group’).median() - Key note: the index labels are the unique values of the grouping key. To make the group keys appear as regular columns, use as_index=False or .reset_index().

Multi-key grouping

Split: data are split by each combination of the keys (e.g., (dept, year)).
Apply: median is computed per numeric column for each (dept, year) group.
Combine: a DataFrame with a MultiIndex (dept, year) is produced.
Code: df.groupby([‘dept’, ‘year’]).median()
Key note: MultiIndex reflects the tuple of grouping keys.

Keeping group keys as columns

Split/Apply/Combine but want keys in columns:
Code: df.groupby(‘group’, as_index=False).median()
Result: columns include ‘group’, along with the medians of numeric columns.

Using different functions for different columns

Split: same as above.
Apply: you apply different aggregations to different columns.
Combine: you get a DataFrame with one row per group and the requested aggregations per column.
Code: df.groupby(‘group’).agg({‘score’: ‘median’, ‘hours’: ‘mean’})

The transform specialization (a SAC variant)

Transform is a special kind of apply that returns data with the same shape as the original (i.e., aligned with the original index). It’s useful when you want to broadcast a per-group statistic back to each row.
Example use cases:

Centering within groups: add a per-row value that is the group’s median subtracted from that row’s score.

Code: df[‘score_centered’] = df[‘score’] - df.groupby(‘team’)[‘score’].transform(‘median’)

Normalizing within groups: (value - group_mean) / group_std

Code: df[‘score_norm’] = (df[‘score’] - df.groupby(‘team’)[‘score’].transform(‘mean’)) / df.groupby(‘team’)[‘score’].transform(‘std’)


Difference from SAC: transform returns values that align with the original rows, not a reduced, group-by result. It’s still split-apply in spirit, but the “combine” step is essentially a broadcast back to the original shape.


Putting it together with your data - Example 1 (single key, median) - Split: by group value - Apply: median on numeric columns - Combine: one row per group, index is the group value - Code: df.groupby(‘group’).median()

Example 2 (two keys, median)

Split: by (dept, year)
Apply: median on numeric columns
Combine: a DataFrame with a MultiIndex (dept, year)
Code: df.groupby([‘dept’, ‘year’]).median()

Example 3 (bring keys back as columns)

Code: df.groupby([‘dept’, ‘year’], as_index=False).median()

Example 4 (transform example)

Center scores within team:
Code: df[‘score_centered’] = df[‘score’] - df.groupby(‘team’)[‘score’].transform(‘median’)
Result: a new column aligned with the original rows, per-group median values broadcast to each row in that group.


Quick recap - SAC: Split data -> Apply a function to each group -> Combine results into a new object (index by group keys). - Transform is a special apply that returns the same shape as the input, useful for per-group normalization or broadcasting. - Your examples fit neatly into SAC: single-key median and multi-key median are splits with median as the apply, then a combined result. If you want the group keys as columns, use as_index=False or reset_index. If you want per-row, per-group values, use transform.
If you want, share a small sample of your data and I’ll walk through exact code for both the SAC and transform patterns on your case. # Selecting and filtering How can I select rows and columns in a single expression without defining an intermediary identifier?


mask = combined_df[[ “female_pct”]] > 0 combined_df[mask][“ay”, “school_name”, “female_pct”]
You can filter rows and select columns in one expression using either .loc or .query. The issue in your code is that you built a mask from a DataFrame slice; you want a boolean Series.

Using .loc (recommended): combined_df.loc[combined_df[‘female_pct’] > 0, [‘ay’, ‘school_name’, ‘female_pct’]]
This keeps rows where female_pct > 0 and returns only the three columns.
Using .query: combined_df.query(‘female_pct > 0’)[[‘ay’, ‘school_name’, ‘female_pct’]]

Notes: - If you only want to drop rows where female_pct is NaN or not positive, the > 0 condition already excludes NaNs. - If you intended to drop rows for male-dominated schools, you’d adjust the threshold to something like female_pct >= 0.5 or another domain-appropriate rule.



Explain .loc[] is primarily label based. Explain index and label and related pandas concepts.
Here’s a concise guide to .loc and the concepts of index and labels in pandas, with a focus on how they’re used for label-based selection.

What .loc is

.loc is a label-based selector for a DataFrame.
It takes two parts: rows selector and columns selector: df.loc[ rows_selector, columns_selector ].
Both selectors are based on the actual labels (not positions).

Core concepts you’ll use

Index (row labels)

The left axis labels of the DataFrame. Accessible as df.index.
Examples of index types: RangeIndex (0,1,2,…), Int64Index, or a DatetimeIndex, or MultiIndex for hierarchical indexing.
The index is used to align and select rows by their labels.

Columns (column labels)

The right axis labels. Accessible as df.columns.
Columns are identified by their names (strings in most dataframes).

Labels vs. positions

Labels are the actual identifier values (e.g., the year, a DBN string, column name).
Positions are the 0-based integer positions (first row is position 0, first column is position 0).
.loc uses labels; .iloc uses integer positions.

Slices with .loc

When you slice with labels, the end label is inclusive (different from Python’s standard end-exclusive slicing).
Example: df.loc[‘2019’:‘2021’, [‘ay’,‘female_pct’]] selects rows with index labels from 2019 through 2021 (inclusive) and the two specified columns.

Boolean masks with .loc

You can pass a boolean Series or array as the row selector: df.loc[mask, cols].
The mask must align with the index length.

Multiple labels

You can pass a list of labels for rows or columns: df.loc[[lbl1, lbl2], [col1, col2]].

Callable with .loc

You can pass a function: df.loc[lambda d: d[‘female_pct’] > 0.2, [‘ay’,‘school_name’,‘female_pct’]].
The function receives the DataFrame and should return a boolean Series or a tuple of (rows, cols) selectors.

MultiIndex

If you have a hierarchical index (MultiIndex), you can index with tuples: df.loc[(‘A’,‘x’), (‘col1’,‘col2’)].

Edge cases

If the index has duplicates, df.loc[label] returns all matching rows (a DataFrame subset).
If a label doesn’t exist, you’ll get a KeyError (unless you use .get with a default, or use .reindex).


Quick practical patterns

Select specific rows by label and specific columns: df.loc[ [‘2019’,‘2020’], [‘ay’,‘female_pct’] ]
Filter rows by a condition and keep certain columns: df.loc[df[‘female_pct’] > 0.2, [‘ay’,‘school_name’,‘female_pct’]]
Range by label (inclusive end) when index is meaningful (e.g., years): df.loc[‘2017’:‘2020’, [‘ay’,‘female_pct’]]
If you know you have a MultiIndex: df.loc[(‘2019’,‘East’), (‘ay’,‘female_pct’)]

Common pitfall to avoid

Do not pass a boolean mask generated from a column to the row selector in .loc unless that mask has the same length as the index. For example, this is correct: df.loc[df[‘female_pct’] > 0.2, [‘ay’,‘school_name’,‘female_pct’]]
But building a mask from a DataFrame slice and using it as the row selector (df.loc[mask, …] where mask is a DataFrame) will raise errors. Use a boolean Series, not a DataFrame, for the row mask.

How this relates to your data in brief

If your index is year-like (e.g., 2017, 2018, …), you can do: df.loc[‘2017’:‘2020’, [‘ay’,‘school_name’,‘female_pct’]]
If you want to select by a row condition (e.g., female_pct > 0.3) and only certain columns: df.loc[df[‘female_pct’] > 0.3, [‘ay’,‘school_name’,‘female_pct’]]


Question to guide the next step - Given a DataFrame where the row index is the year and the columns include ay, school_name, and female_pct, how would you select all rows from 2018 to 2020 and only the three columns listed? Would you prefer a label-based slice or a boolean mask approach, and why?
Why is the nomenclature ‘label’? I think you are saying that .loc[] selects by data value rather than index? What is the significance of the term ‘label’?
Short answer - If your row index is the year and you want 2018–2020 with the three columns, use a label-based slice: - df.loc[‘2018’:‘2020’, [‘ay’, ‘school_name’, ‘female_pct’]] - If your index is numeric, df.loc[2018:2020, [‘ay’, ‘school_name’, ‘female_pct’]] also works (and is inclusive on the end).

If you want to filter by a condition (e.g., female_pct > 0) and keep those columns:

df.loc[df[‘female_pct’] > 0, [‘ay’, ‘school_name’, ‘female_pct’]]


Notes on your mask - Build the mask as a boolean Series, not a DataFrame: - mask = df[‘female_pct’] > 0 - df.loc[mask, [‘ay’,‘school_name’,‘female_pct’]]
What “.loc[]” means and what “label” means - What .loc[] is: - .loc is a label-based selector. It selects by the actual labels on the index (rows) and the column names (columns). - It takes two parts: rows_selector and columns_selector: df.loc[rows_selector, columns_selector]. - Both selectors are based on labels, not on integer positions.

What “label” refers to:

A label is the identifier used on an axis to refer to a row or a column. For rows, these are the index values (e.g., 2018, 2019, ‘East High School’). For columns, these are the column names (e.g., ‘ay’, ‘school_name’, ‘female_pct’).
Labels are how you reference data in .loc. They are not the actual data values inside cells.

Why this distinction matters:

.loc uses the actual index/column labels to pick data, which makes it readable and aligned with your data’s identifiers.
.iloc would use integer positions (0-based), which is different from the label values.
Using a boolean mask with .loc requires the mask to align with the index (a Series of True/False with the same length as the index). A mask created from a DataFrame slice (which is itself a DataFrame) won’t work.


Quick recap patterns - Label-based row range + selected columns: - df.loc[‘2018’:‘2020’, [‘ay’, ‘school_name’, ‘female_pct’]] - Boolean-filtered rows + selected columns: - df.loc[df[‘female_pct’] > 0, [‘ay’,‘school_name’,‘female_pct’]]

title: Groupby
text:
mask = combined_df[[ “female_pct”]] > 0 combined_df[mask][“ay”, “school_name”, “female_pct”]
You can filter rows and select columns in one expression using either .loc or .query. The issue in your code is that you built a mask from a DataFrame slice; you want a boolean Series.

Using .loc (recommended): combined_df.loc[combined_df[‘female_pct’] > 0, [‘ay’, ‘school_name’, ‘female_pct’]]
This keeps rows where female_pct > 0 and returns only the three columns.
Using .query: combined_df.query(‘female_pct > 0’)[[‘ay’, ‘school_name’, ‘female_pct’]]

Notes: - If you only want to drop rows where female_pct is NaN or not positive, the > 0 condition already excludes NaNs. - If you intended to drop rows for male-dominated schools, you’d adjust the threshold to something like female_pct >= 0.5 or another domain-appropriate rule.

title: Groupby
text:
Explain .loc[] is primarily label based. Explain index and label and related pandas concepts.
Here’s a concise guide to .loc and the concepts of index and labels in pandas, with a focus on how they’re used for label-based selection.

What .loc is

.loc is a label-based selector for a DataFrame.
It takes two parts: rows selector and columns selector: df.loc[ rows_selector, columns_selector ].
Both selectors are based on the actual labels (not positions).

Core concepts you’ll use

Index (row labels)

The left axis labels of the DataFrame. Accessible as df.index.
Examples of index types: RangeIndex (0,1,2,…), Int64Index, or a DatetimeIndex, or MultiIndex for hierarchical indexing.
The index is used to align and select rows by their labels.

Columns (column labels)

The right axis labels. Accessible as df.columns.
Columns are identified by their names (strings in most dataframes).

Labels vs. positions

Labels are the actual identifier values (e.g., the year, a DBN string, column name).
Positions are the 0-based integer positions (first row is position 0, first column is position 0).
.loc uses labels; .iloc uses integer positions.

Slices with .loc

When you slice with labels, the end label is inclusive (different from Python’s standard end-exclusive slicing).
Example: df.loc[‘2019’:‘2021’, [‘ay’,‘female_pct’]] selects rows with index labels from 2019 through 2021 (inclusive) and the two specified columns.

Boolean masks with .loc

You can pass a boolean Series or array as the row selector: df.loc[mask, cols].
The mask must align with the index length.

Multiple labels

You can pass a list of labels for rows or columns: df.loc[[lbl1, lbl2], [col1, col2]].

Callable with .loc

You can pass a function: df.loc[lambda d: d[‘female_pct’] > 0.2, [‘ay’,‘school_name’,‘female_pct’]].
The function receives the DataFrame and should return a boolean Series or a tuple of (rows, cols) selectors.

MultiIndex

If you have a hierarchical index (MultiIndex), you can index with tuples: df.loc[(‘A’,‘x’), (‘col1’,‘col2’)].

Edge cases

If the index has duplicates, df.loc[label] returns all matching rows (a DataFrame subset).
If a label doesn’t exist, you’ll get a KeyError (unless you use .get with a default, or use .reindex).


Quick practical patterns

Select specific rows by label and specific columns: df.loc[ [‘2019’,‘2020’], [‘ay’,‘female_pct’] ]
Filter rows by a condition and keep certain columns: df.loc[df[‘female_pct’] > 0.2, [‘ay’,‘school_name’,‘female_pct’]]
Range by label (inclusive end) when index is meaningful (e.g., years): df.loc[‘2017’:‘2020’, [‘ay’,‘female_pct’]]
If you know you have a MultiIndex: df.loc[(‘2019’,‘East’), (‘ay’,‘female_pct’)]

Common pitfall to avoid

Do not pass a boolean mask generated from a column to the row selector in .loc unless that mask has the same length as the index. For example, this is correct: df.loc[df[‘female_pct’] > 0.2, [‘ay’,‘school_name’,‘female_pct’]]
But building a mask from a DataFrame slice and using it as the row selector (df.loc[mask, …] where mask is a DataFrame) will raise errors. Use a boolean Series, not a DataFrame, for the row mask.

How this relates to your data in brief

If your index is year-like (e.g., 2017, 2018, …), you can do: df.loc[‘2017’:‘2020’, [‘ay’,‘school_name’,‘female_pct’]]
If you want to select by a row condition (e.g., female_pct > 0.3) and only certain columns: df.loc[df[‘female_pct’] > 0.3, [‘ay’,‘school_name’,‘female_pct’]]


Question to guide the next step - Given a DataFrame where the row index is the year and the columns include ay, school_name, and female_pct, how would you select all rows from 2018 to 2020 and only the three columns listed? Would you prefer a label-based slice or a boolean mask approach, and why?
Why is the nomenclature ‘label’? I think you are saying that .loc[] selects by data value rather than index? What is the significance of the term ‘label’?
Short answer - If your row index is the year and you want 2018–2020 with the three columns, use a label-based slice: - df.loc[‘2018’:‘2020’, [‘ay’, ‘school_name’, ‘female_pct’]] - If your index is numeric, df.loc[2018:2020, [‘ay’, ‘school_name’, ‘female_pct’]] also works (and is inclusive on the end).

If you want to filter by a condition (e.g., female_pct > 0) and keep those columns:

df.loc[df[‘female_pct’] > 0, [‘ay’, ‘school_name’, ‘female_pct’]]


Notes on your mask - Build the mask as a boolean Series, not a DataFrame: - mask = df[‘female_pct’] > 0 - df.loc[mask, [‘ay’,‘school_name’,‘female_pct’]]
What “.loc[]” means and what “label” means - What .loc[] is: - .loc is a label-based selector. It selects by the actual labels on the index (rows) and the column names (columns). - It takes two parts: rows_selector and columns_selector: df.loc[rows_selector, columns_selector]. - Both selectors are based on labels, not on integer positions.

What “label” refers to:

A label is the identifier used on an axis to refer to a row or a column. For rows, these are the index values (e.g., 2018, 2019, ‘East High School’). For columns, these are the column names (e.g., ‘ay’, ‘school_name’, ‘female_pct’).
Labels are how you reference data in .loc. They are not the actual data values inside cells.

Why this distinction matters:

.loc uses the actual index/column labels to pick data, which makes it readable and aligned with your data’s identifiers.
.iloc would use integer positions (0-based), which is different from the label values.
Using a boolean mask with .loc requires the mask to align with the index (a Series of True/False with the same length as the index). A mask created from a DataFrame slice (which is itself a DataFrame) won’t work.


Quick recap patterns - Label-based row range + selected columns: - df.loc[‘2018’:‘2020’, [‘ay’, ‘school_name’, ‘female_pct’]] - Boolean-filtered rows + selected columns: - df.loc[df[‘female_pct’] > 0, [‘ay’,‘school_name’,‘female_pct’]]

        str_detect(col, "grade9geapplicantsperseat\\d+$") ~ "grade9geapplicantsperseat",
        str_detect(col, "^seats9ge\\d$")                  ~ "seats9ge",
        str_detect(col, "^requirement_\\d_\\d+$")        ~ "requirement",
        str_detect(col, "^program\\d+$")                 ~ "program",
        str_detect(col, "^grade9swdapplicatsperseat\\d+$") ~ "grade9swdapplicatsperseat",
        str(col, "^grade9swdseatsperseat\\d+$") ~ "grade9swdseatsperseat",
        str_detect(col, "^grade9swdpriority\\d+$") ~ "grade9swdpriority",
        str_detect(col, "^grade9gepriority\\d+$") ~ "grade9gepriority",
        
        TRUE ~ "other"
      )
    )
    cols_tbl %>% count(family, sort=TRUE)
}

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
User: I need a survey of strategies for handling wide tibble with blocks or families of data, which I will compress into a long table for program level questions. I’ve started manually building the mapping to the families by inspecting a helper table of column names. I want to learn additional techniques, but as always am looking for incremental learning. Nonetheless be sure to include the most sophisticated and seasoned professional statistitions solution along with the near field suggestions.
Positron Assistant: For this kind of data, the core problem is usually:
A useful way to think about strategies is by maturity level.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
1. Manual family mapping by inspection
This is what you’ve started doing. It is a good first step.
Typical workflow:

list all column names
sort by length or pattern
identify repeated families
write regex rules for each family
pivot those families into long form

This is slow but valuable because it teaches the schema.
Your current classify_families() is the right instinct. A few issues in it:

str() should be str_detect()
some patterns are not anchored consistently
you probably want to extract a program index, not only assign a family
a family table is more useful if it includes both family and index

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
2. Pattern inventory table
A stronger next step is to create a metadata table of all columns and parse their structure before pivoting anything.
This is often the first professional move because it separates:

schema understanding
transformation logic

You can build a table like:

col
family
program_index
is_program_level
base_name
notes

Example:

library(dplyr)


Attaching package: 'dplyr'


The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':

    filter, lag


The following objects are masked from 'package:base':

    intersect, setdiff, setequal, union

library(stringr)
library(tidyr)
library(tibble)

build_column_dictionary <- function(data) {
  tibble(col = names(data)) |>
    mutate(
      family = case_when(
        str_detect(col, "^program\\d+$") ~ "program",
        str_detect(col, "^seats9ge\\d+$") ~ "seats9ge",
        str_detect(col, "^grade9geapplicantsperseat\\d+$") ~ "grade9geapplicantsperseat",
        str_detect(col, "^grade9swdapplicatsperseat\\d+$") ~ "grade9swdapplicatsperseat",
        str_detect(col, "^grade9swdseatsperseat\\d+$") ~ "grade9swdseatsperseat",
        str_detect(col, "^grade9swdpriority\\d+$") ~ "grade9swdpriority",
        str_detect(col, "^grade9gepriority\\d+$") ~ "grade9gepriority",
        str_detect(col, "^requirement_\\d+_\\d+$") ~ "requirement",
        TRUE ~ "other"
      ),
      program_index = case_when(
        str_detect(col, "\\d+$") ~ str_extract(col, "\\d+$"),
        str_detect(col, "^requirement_\\d+_\\d+$") ~ str_match(col, "^requirement_(\\d+)_(\\d+)$")[, 2],
        TRUE ~ NA_character_
      ),
      sub_index = case_when(
        str_detect(col, "^requirement_\\d+_\\d+$") ~ str_match(col, "^requirement_(\\d+)_(\\d+)$")[, 3],
        TRUE ~ NA_character_
      ),
      is_program_level = family != "other"
    )
}

This gives you a reusable schema object.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
3. Regex-driven pivoting with .value
Once families are understood, the most effective tidyverse approach is usually pivot_longer() with names_pattern and .value.
This is the standard “wide repeated blocks to long” solution when names are regular enough.
If names looked like:

program1, program2
seats9ge1, seats9ge2
grade9gepriority1, grade9gepriority2

then you can reshape many families at once.
Example:
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(stringr)

program_long <- hs_directory |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = matches("^(program|seats9ge|grade9gepriority|grade9swdpriority|grade9geapplicantsperseat|grade9swdapplicatsperseat|grade9swdseatsperseat)\\d+$"),
    names_to = c(".value", "program_index"),
    names_pattern = "^(.*?)(\\d+)$"
  ) |>
  mutate(program_index = as.integer(program_index))
Why this is strong:

one pivot handles multiple families
the suffix becomes program_index
family names become output columns automatically

This is usually the best next step if your schema is mostly regular.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
4. Family-by-family pivots, then join
If the naming conventions are messy, a more robust approach is to pivot each family separately and join them by school keys plus program_index.
This is less elegant, but often safer.
Why professionals do this:

each family can have its own parsing rule
easier to debug
easier to validate row counts
easier to handle malformed exceptions

Pattern:
id_cols <- c("dbn", "school_name")

program_tbl <- hs_directory |>
  select(all_of(id_cols), matches("^program\\d+$")) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = matches("^program\\d+$"),
    names_to = "program_index",
    names_pattern = "^program(\\d+)$",
    values_to = "program"
  )

seats_tbl <- hs_directory |>
  select(all_of(id_cols), matches("^seats9ge\\d+$")) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = matches("^seats9ge\\d+$"),
    names_to = "program_index",
    names_pattern = "^seats9ge(\\d+)$",
    values_to = "seats9ge"
  )

program_long <- program_tbl |>
  left_join(seats_tbl, join_by(dbn, school_name, program_index))
This is often the right middle ground for real administrative data.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
5. Spec table + generated reshape plan
A more seasoned approach is to make the schema explicit as data: a “pivot spec” table.
Instead of hardcoding regex inside transformation code, you create a tibble describing each family:

family name
regex
value column name
whether it is indexed by program only
whether it has a second sub-index
optional cleaning function

Example:
library(tibble)

family_spec <- tribble(
  ~family,                      ~pattern,                                      ~value_name,                    ~kind,
  "program",                    "^program(\\d+)$",                             "program",                      "program",
  "seats9ge",                   "^seats9ge(\\d+)$",                            "seats9ge",                     "program",
  "grade9geapplicantsperseat",  "^grade9geapplicantsperseat(\\d+)$",           "grade9geapplicantsperseat",    "program",
  "grade9swdapplicatsperseat",  "^grade9swdapplicatsperseat(\\d+)$",           "grade9swdapplicatsperseat",    "program",
  "grade9swdpriority",          "^grade9swdpriority(\\d+)$",                   "grade9swdpriority",            "program",
  "grade9gepriority",           "^grade9gepriority(\\d+)$",                    "grade9gepriority",             "program",
  "requirement",                "^requirement_(\\d+)_(\\d+)$",                 "requirement",                  "program_sub"
)
Then write functions that consume this spec.
Benefits:

transformation is declarative
easier to audit
easier to extend
better for messy public-sector datasets that evolve over time

This is much closer to how an experienced data engineer or statistical programmer would stabilize the pipeline.

title: My recommendation by stage
section: 6. Nested / list-column strategy for multi-valued families
text:
6. Nested / list-column strategy for multi-valued families
Some families, like requirement_1_1, requirement_1_2, etc., are not really scalar program attributes. They are repeated values within program.
In that case, forcing them into one flat program row too early can make the model awkward.
Better pattern:

first create one row per school-program
keep repeated-within-program families in nested child tables or list-columns
unnest only when needed

Example mental model:

school_tbl: one row per school
program_tbl: one row per school-program
requirement_tbl: one row per school-program-requirement

This is often the most correct relational design.
For example:
requirement_long <- hs_directory |>
  select(dbn, matches("^requirement_\\d+_\\d+$")) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = matches("^requirement_\\d+_\\d+$"),
    names_to = c("program_index", "requirement_index"),
    names_pattern = "^requirement_(\\d+)_(\\d+)$",
    values_to = "requirement"
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(requirement), requirement != "")
This is better than trying to paste all requirements into a single field unless your downstream questions truly need that.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
7. Relational normalization, not just pivoting
The most seasoned professional solution is usually not “make one big long table.”
It is:

identify the unit of observation for each analytical question
normalize the source into several linked tables
validate keys and cardinality
only denormalize for specific outputs

For your case, likely target tables are:

schools

school-level attributes
one row per dbn

programs

one row per dbn + program_index
program name, seats, applicants per seat, priorities

program_requirements

one row per dbn + program_index + requirement_index

maybe program_priorities too, if priorities are also repeated categories rather than a single scalar

Why this is the senior solution:

matches the data-generating structure
easier to test
less information loss
avoids giant fragile pivots
works better when some families have different multiplicities

Statisticians working with institutional data often arrive here because “one tidy table” is not always the best storage form. Tidy analysis tables are often downstream products, not the canonical cleaned data model.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
8. Use a name parser function
Another strong technique is to stop thinking in terms of family-specific regex scattered everywhere and instead write a parser for column names.
Example output for each column:

program12 -> family = program, index = 12
seats9ge4 -> family = seats9ge, index = 4
requirement_3_2 -> family = requirement, index = 3, sub_index = 2

Example:
parse_hs_column <- function(col) {
  case_when(
    str_detect(col, "^program\\d+$") ~ "program",
    str_detect(col, "^seats9ge\\d+$") ~ "seats9ge",
    str_detect(col, "^grade9geapplicantsperseat\\d+$") ~ "grade9geapplicantsperseat",
    str_detect(col, "^requirement_\\d+_\\d+$") ~ "requirement",
    TRUE ~ "other"
  )
}
More sophisticated version returns a tibble row, not just a label.
This becomes the foundation for:

schema reports
validation
family counts
selecting pivotable columns
generating documentation

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
9. Validation-first workflow
A very professional addition is to validate structure before and after reshaping.
Examples of checks:

maximum observed program index by school
whether program3 is populated when program2 is missing
whether all family members share the same set of indices
whether program rows with missing program name should be dropped
whether requirements exist for nonexistent programs

Useful checks:
hs_directory |>
  transmute(
    dbn,
    n_program_cols = rowSums(!is.na(select(cur_data(), matches("^program\\d+$"))))
  )
And after pivoting:

unique key check on dbn + program_index
row count by school
missingness profile by family

The professional habit is: do not trust the wide structure just because the names look regular.

title: My recommendation by stage
section: 10. “Schema dictionary first” as a Quarto artifact
text:
10. “Schema dictionary first” as a Quarto artifact
Since you are already working in Quarto, a very useful incremental practice is to produce a column family audit table as part of the notebook.
That artifact can include:

family counts
unmatched columns
parsed indices
example names from each family

This is low effort and high value.
Example summary:
column_dict <- build_column_dictionary(hs_directory)

family_summary <- column_dict |>
  count(family, is_program_level, sort = TRUE)

family_examples <- column_dict |>
  filter(family != "other") |>
  summarize(
    example_cols = paste(head(col, 5), collapse = ", "),
    .by = family
  )
That gives you a documented schema exploration layer before transformation.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
Near-field next step
Do this next:

build a proper column dictionary
parse family, program_index, and sub_index
pivot only the clean one-index families first
keep requirement_*_* as a separate long table

That is incremental and teaches the right concepts.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
Strong intermediate solution
Use:

one dictionary table
one pivot_longer(..., names_to = c(".value", "program_index")) for regular families
separate pivots for irregular families
joins on school id + program index

This is likely the best balance of learning and practicality.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
Most seasoned professional solution
Treat this as normalization, not just reshaping.
Build a small schema system:

a column dictionary
a family spec table
parsing functions
validation checks
output relational tables:

schools
programs
program_requirements


That is the solution I would trust for repeated use, auditing, and future extension.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
Pass 1
Only create and inspect the dictionary table.
Questions to answer:

which families exist?
which are unmatched?
what indices exist?
are there gaps?

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
Pass 2
Build programs from the simple one-index families only.
Questions to answer:

what is the key?
how many programs per school?
which rows are empty shell programs?

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
Pass 3
Add requirements as a child table.
Questions to answer:

is requirement count variable by program?
do requirement rows exist for missing programs?

If you want, I can sketch a concrete schools / programs / program_requirements pipeline for your hs_directory schema using tidyverse only.

Positron Assistant: Yes. With the full column list, I’d confirm the recommendation and sharpen it.
This dataset is not just “wide.” It is a mixed-grain table containing at least three structural levels:

school level
program level
program sub-record level for repeated fields like requirements and priorities

That means the most seasoned strategy is indeed a small normalized model, not a single giant long table.

title: My recommendation by stage
section: What the full column list confirms
text:
What the full column list confirms
The columns break pretty cleanly into these groups.

1. School-level columns
These appear to be one-per-school attributes:

identifiers: dbn, school_name, boro, city, zip, building_code
contact/location: website, url, phone_number, fax_number, school_email, location, primary_address_line_1
operations: start_time, end_time, gradespan, shared_space, transfer, international, specialized, earlycollege, ptech, pbat, girls, boys
descriptive text: overview_paragraph, extracurricular_activities, advancedplacement_courses, language_classes, academicopportunities1 to academicopportunities6, freshmanschedule, school_accessibility_description, diversity_in_admissions
metrics: graduation_rate, attendance_rate, college_career_rate, pct_stu_safe, pct_stu_enough_variety, total_students
programs/support/offers: school_sports, psal_sports_boys, psal_sports_girls, psal_sports_coed, ell_programs, finalgrades, diplomaendorsements
other site-like fields: recruitment_website, sqr_website
transit: subway, bus
neighborhood-ish: neighborhood, campus_name

These should stay in a schools table.


2. Program-level repeated blocks
These clearly repeat over program index, mostly 1 through 11:

program1 … program11
code1 … code11
interest1 … interest11
method1 … method11
eligibility1 … eligibility11
prgdesc1 … prgdesc11
auditioninformation1 … auditioninformation11
common_audition1 … common_audition11
seats9ge1 … seats9ge11
seats9swd1 … seats9swd11
seats101 … seats1011
grade9geapplicants1 … grade9geapplicants11
grade9swdapplicants1 … grade9swdapplicants11
grade9geapplicantsperseat1 … 11
grade9swdapplicantsperseat1 … 11
grade9gefilledflag1 … 11
grade9swdfilledflag1 … 11

These belong in a programs table keyed by dbn + program_index.


3. Program-level but nonstandard repeated blocks
These are still program-level, but naming is less regular:

priority1_prog1, priority2_prog1, priority3_prog1, etc.
requirement_1_1, requirement_2_1, requirement_3_1, requirement_4_1, etc.
specialized-specific fields:

applicants1specialized … applicants6specialized
appperseat1specialized … appperseat6specialized
seats1specialized … seats6specialized


These confirm that one long table would either:

become sparse and awkward, or
force lossy flattening

So I would not recommend a single canonical long table.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
Important structural observation
There are actually multiple program-like systems in the same sheet:

A. General school program blocks
Indexed by 1 to 11, using names like:

program{n}
code{n}
interest{n}



B. Priority subrecords within program
Indexed by both:

priority rank
program index

Example:

priority1_prog10
priority2_prog10
priority3_prog4

This is a separate child table: program_priorities
with key: - dbn - program_index - priority_rank


C. Requirement subrecords within program
Indexed by both:

requirement rank
program index

Example: - requirement_1_10 - requirement_2_10 - requirement_4_1
This is another child table: program_requirements
with key: - dbn - program_index - requirement_index


D. Specialized admissions block
These look like a different subsystem, likely not aligned to the ordinary program1 to program11 block:

applicants1specialized
appperseat1specialized
seats1specialized

I would treat these cautiously. They may need their own table: specialized_tracks or specialized_programs
rather than being forced into the ordinary program index.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
Confirmed recommendation: normalized target model
I would now recommend these output tables.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
1. schools
One row per dbn.
Contains only non-indexed columns.
Candidate key: - dbn

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
2. school_academic_opportunities
Because academicopportunities1 to academicopportunities6 are repeated but not obviously program-indexed.
Key: - dbn - opportunity_index
This is optional, but cleaner than six columns.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
3. programs
One row per school-program block.
Key: - dbn - program_index
Fields likely include:

program
code
interest
method
eligibility
prgdesc
auditioninformation
common_audition
seats9ge
seats9swd
seats10
grade9geapplicants
grade9swdapplicants
grade9geapplicantsperseat
grade9swdapplicantsperseat
grade9gefilledflag
grade9swdfilledflag

This is your main analysis-ready program table.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
4. program_priorities
One row per priority attached to a program.
Key: - dbn - program_index - priority_rank
Value: - priority_text

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
5. program_requirements
One row per requirement attached to a program.
Key: - dbn - program_index - requirement_index
Value: - requirement_text

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
6. specialized_programs or specialized_admissions
One row per specialized track index.
Key: - dbn - specialized_index
Fields: - specialized_applicants - specialized_applicants_per_seat - specialized_seats
I would keep this separate until you verify how it maps to the named school programs.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
School grain
Columns with no trailing program index and no embedded sub-index.
Examples: - dbn - school_name - city - zip - attendance_rate - overview_paragraph

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
Program grain, suffix index pattern
Pattern: - ^(family)([0-9]+)$
Examples: - program10 - code3 - seats9ge7 - grade9gefilledflag11

title: My recommendation by stage
section: Program child grain, embedded rank + program index
text:
Program child grain, embedded rank + program index
Pattern: - ^priority([0-9]+)_prog([0-9]+)$ - ^requirement_([0-9]+)_([0-9]+)$
These need two indices.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
Specialized grain
Pattern: - ^(applicants|appperseat|seats)([0-9]+)specialized$
This is its own indexed family.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
Repeated school child grain
Pattern: - ^academicopportunities([0-9]+)$
These are repeated school-level descriptors, not program descriptors.
That means your parser should extract:

grain
family
index_1
index_2

not just family.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
1. seats101 to seats1011
This family is easy to misread.
These likely mean: - seats10 for program 1 - seats10 for program 11
not: - seat count 101, 102, etc.
So this family should probably be parsed as:

family = seats10
program_index = 1 to 11

That means regex must prefer the known family prefix, not just “split trailing digits.”

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
2. Missing maximum priority count
Some programs have up to priority3, others only priority2.
So program_priorities must allow varying row counts by program.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
3. Missing maximum requirement count
Some programs have up to requirement_4_1, others only two.
Same point: this is a child table, not a fixed-width attribute block.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
4. academicopportunities1 to 6
These may be better collapsed into a single list/string for reporting, but for canonical cleaned data I’d normalize them.

title: My recommendation by stage
section: 5. Specialized admissions may not map one-to-one to program{n}

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
Phase 1: schema dictionary
Build a dictionary with columns:

col
grain
family
index_1
index_2
parse_status

Where examples would be:

program10 -> grain = "program", family = "program", index_1 = 10
priority2_prog4 -> grain = "program_priority", family = "priority", index_1 = 4, index_2 = 2
requirement_3_1 -> grain = "program_requirement", family = "requirement", index_1 = 1, index_2 = 3
academicopportunities5 -> grain = "school_opportunity", family = "academicopportunities", index_1 = 5

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
Phase 2: normalized tables
Produce:

schools
school_academic_opportunities
programs
program_priorities
program_requirements
specialized_programs

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
Phase 3: validation
Check:

dbn unique in schools
dbn + program_index unique in programs
all priority rows match an existing program
all requirement rows match an existing program
no orphan specialized rows if you later map them

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
Phase 4: analysis marts
Only then create convenience tables like:

programs_with_school_context
programs_with_priority_summary
programs_with_requirement_summary

These are denormalized products for analysis, not the canonical cleaned data.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
Step 1
Write the parser and dictionary.
This is the most important step.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
Step 2
Build only programs.
Ignore priorities, requirements, specialized for one pass.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
Step 3
Build program_priorities and program_requirements.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
Step 4
Handle academicopportunities.

title: My recommendation by stage
text:
Step 5
Inspect specialized fields separately and decide whether to integrate or keep separate.
That preserves learning while still following the senior architecture.

title: first post
text:
Embarking
This learning blog marks a beginning. Something of a formal step toward transitioning to a new career. My experience as a NYC computer science teacher and school spreadsheet builder has given me a foundation in institutional education and computing. I seek to build on the combined foundation toward education data systems and analysis. My hope is to find sustainability in a career that calls upon me as a problem solver, communicator, and mathematician.

title: About
text: This site is a learning project. It is early work along a pathway targeting institutional education data analysis. This project will focus on showcasing the learning The technology stack is Python + Jupyter, R + knitr, Postgresql with psycopg2, and Quarto for publishing. The learning plan is in collaboration with various Claude models. On going learning questions are answered by ChatGPT 5 Nano. The work itself is hand written toward the goal of strong interview performance on technical, statistical, and institutional/political topics. Future work will focus on NYC school quality models and distributions of the new College and Career Readiness Metric.

title: Lessons from flight package
text:
Lessons from flight package
Vectors, Factors and Flight
Factor Vectors are an important concept. Factor levels. Order of a factor. Factors seem like objects with properties. A factor has an internal vector c(…) and other meta data such as levels=… and ordered=… factor(name=c(…), …=…, ) Only store unique values.

factor(c('a', 'b', 'c'),
#levels=, 
#labels=, 
exclude=NA, 
ordered=is.ordered(x=c('c', 'b', 'a')),nmax=NA)

[1] a b c
Levels: a b c


Create a set of indicator or ‘dummy’ variables using model.matrix. One colomn for each level of the factor.

nf <- factor(c('a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g', 'h', 'i', 'j'))

title: Data Science Learning Resources
text:
A curated library of learning resources for a mid-career transition into data science. This collection emphasizes foundations first, then practical workflows in R and Python for data munging, ETL, statistical inference, modeling, communication, and reproducible publishing.



Start with foundations: probability, statistics, modeling, and computational thinking.
Build fluency in one primary language first (R or Python), then learn the other.
Practice on small, well-understood datasets before moving to larger and messier data.
Learn the full workflow: ingest, clean, explore, model, communicate, and reproduce.
Publish your work regularly with notebooks, reports, dashboards, and version control.





Statistical foundations: uncertainty, variation, estimation, testing, regression.
Programming foundations: data structures, functions, iteration, debugging, packages.
Data wrangling: tabular data, joins, reshaping, missingness, dates, strings.
Visualization and communication: exploratory graphics, explanatory graphics, reports.
Modeling: linear models, classification, resampling, regularization, evaluation.
Data engineering basics: ETL/ELT, SQL, validation, pipelines, containers, environments.
Publishing and reproducibility: Quarto, package/project management, containers, CI.







Freedman, Pisani, Purves — Statistics

Clear conceptual treatment of statistical reasoning.
Strong for sampling, bias, uncertainty, and interpretation.

George Casella, Roger L. Berger — Statistical Inference

Standard mathematical treatment of inference.

This document is intentionally broad. A good next step is to tailor it into a 12-week or 6-month curriculum with weekly readings, exercises, and portfolio milestones aligned to your background and target roles (analyst, data scientist, analytics engineer, research scientist, or BI developer).

title: Data Science Learning Resources
text:
Start with foundations: probability, statistics, modeling, and computational thinking.
Build fluency in one primary language first (R or Python), then learn the other.
Practice on small, well-understood datasets before moving to larger and messier data.
Learn the full workflow: ingest, clean, explore, model, communicate, and reproduce.
Publish your work regularly with notebooks, reports, dashboards, and version control.

title: Data Science Learning Resources
text:
Statistical foundations: uncertainty, variation, estimation, testing, regression.
Programming foundations: data structures, functions, iteration, debugging, packages.
Data wrangling: tabular data, joins, reshaping, missingness, dates, strings.
Visualization and communication: exploratory graphics, explanatory graphics, reports.
Modeling: linear models, classification, resampling, regularization, evaluation.
Data engineering basics: ETL/ELT, SQL, validation, pipelines, containers, environments.
Publishing and reproducibility: Quarto, package/project management, containers, CI.
